content.full_text:
k

y

RAVI MEHTA
CHAIRMAN

FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION

April 2, 1997

Ms. Ann R. Danforth
Town Attorney
Town of Tiburon
1155 Tiburon Boulevard
Tiburon, California 94920

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-96-342a

Dear Ms. Danforth:

You have asked for reconsideration of advice provided to you in Letter No. A-96-342 on
behalf of Ms. Terry Hennessy, mayor of the Town of Tiburon, regarding the conflict-of-interest
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

We previously advised and reaffirm that Mayor Hennessy must disqualify herself from
decisions regarding the future use of the old Safeway property because her residence is located
135 feet from the property and decisions about the use of the property would have a financial
effect on her real property interest. Facts about Mayor Hennessy's condominium unit and the
Safeway property are described in Letter No. A-96-342. You have submitted additional facts
which we have considered regarding the purchase price of Mayor Hennessy's unit, its probable
fair market value, and the desirability of such condominium units as evidenced by the availability
of buyers for similar units.

You argue that Mayor Hennessy should be able to participate in decisions regarding the
use of the Safeway property located 135 feet from her condominium residence because such
decisions are not likely to affect the price that Mayor Hennessy would be able to realize if she
sold her condominium unit, as the unit is subject to resale restrictions as part of an affordable
housing program (described in Letter No. A-96-342). You submit that "there is no substantial
likelihood that a decision regarding the Safeway will have any financial effect on Mayor
Hennessy." You state that "under Government Code Section 87103, the bar of the Political
Reform Act is raised only where it is reasonably foreseeable that a particular decision will have a
material financial effect on the official."

¹ Government Code sections 81000 - 91015. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18000 -
18995, of the California Code of Regulations.

428 J STREET, SUITE 800, SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814 (916) 322-5660

File No. A-96-342a
Page 2

However, the Act does not merely restrict officials from acting if a decision has a material financial effect on the official personally. Section 87100 provides that a public official may not make or participate in making a governmental decision in which the knows or has reason to know he has a financial interest. Section 87103 provides in pertinent part as follows:

"A public official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a member of his or her immediate family, or on any of the following:

(a) Any business entity in which the public official has a direct or indirect investment worth one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more.

(b) Any real property in which the public official has a direct or indirect interest worth one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more.

(c) Any source of income, other than gifts and other than loans by a commercial lending institution in the regular course of business on terms available to the public without regard to official status, aggregating two hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in value provided to, received by or promised to the public official within 12 months prior to the time when the decision is made.

(d) Any business entity in which the public official is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of management.

(e) Any donor of, or any intermediary or agent for a donor of, a gift or gifts aggregating two hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in value provided to, received by, or promised to the public official within 12 months prior to the time when the decision is made.

* * * *

Clearly, the Act contemplates that officials will be disqualified from participating in decisions if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on an economic interest of the official's. In this case, it is reasonably foreseeable that decisions about the property will have a financial effect on real property in which Mayor Hennessy has an interest under Section 87103(b). As discussed in Letter No. A-96-342, if the real property in which the official has an interest is located within 300 feet of the property that is the subject of the decision, the decision is considered material and the official may not participate unless the decision would have no financial effect on the official's real property interest. (Regulation 18702.3(a)(1).)

File No. A-96-342a
Page 3

Despite the resale restrictions on her unit, decisions regarding the use of the Safeway property located 135 feet from Mayor Hennessy's residence would have some financial effect on the fair market value of her real property interest. As discussed in Letter No. A-96-342, if an anchor grocery store leases the Safeway building, or an attractive neighborhood shopping center is developed on the property, the fair market value of Mayor Hennessy's condominium unit would be enhanced. Alternatively, if the property remains vacant or is leased for less desirable uses, the fair market value of her property would decrease. Under Sections 87100 and 87103(b), and Regulation 18702.3(a)(1), Mayor Hennessy may not participate in decisions regarding the Safeway property which is located 135 feet from her residence.

If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel

By: Hyla P. Wagner
Staff Counsel, Legal Division

SGC:HPW:ak

sections.conclusion: Mayor Hennessy may not participate in decisions regarding the Safeway property located 135 feet from her residence. Despite resale restrictions on her condominium unit, decisions about the Safeway property's use would reasonably foreseeably have a material financial effect on the fair market value of her real property interest under Government Code Sections 87100 and 87103(b) and Regulation 18702.3(a)(1).
sections.question_synthetic: Must Mayor Hennessy disqualify herself from decisions regarding the Safeway property located 135 feet from her residence, given that her condominium unit is subject to resale restrictions under an affordable housing program that may insulate it from financial effects of those decisions?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC reaffirms its prior advice that Mayor Hennessy must disqualify herself because decisions about the nearby Safeway property would foreseeably have a material financial effect on her real property interest, regardless of resale restrictions on her unit. The proximity (135 feet) combined with potential effects on fair market value triggers the disqualification requirement under Political Reform Act provisions.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is a response to a reconsideration request (File A-96-342a) on a prior advice letter (A-96-342). Document contains detailed statutory citations and regulatory analysis but no formal Q/C section headers. Conclusion is synthesized from the substantive analysis in the letter body.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Must Mayor Hennessy disqualify herself from decisions regarding the Safeway property located 135 feet from her residence, given that her condominium unit is subject to resale restrictions under an affordable housing program that may insulate it from financial effects of those decisions?

CONCLUSION: Mayor Hennessy may not participate in decisions regarding the Safeway property located 135 feet from her residence. Despite resale restrictions on her condominium unit, decisions about the Safeway property's use would reasonably foreseeably have a material financial effect on the fair market value of her real property interest under Government Code Sections 87100 and 87103(b) and Regulation 18702.3(a)(1).

embedding.summary: The FPPC responds to a reconsideration request regarding Mayor Hennessy's disqualification from decisions on the Safeway property, reaffirming that she must disqualify herself because such decisions would foreseeably have a material financial effect on her nearby condominium, despite affordable housing resale restrictions.
